description: Canonical eval cases for the Finance Agent. Pins payment-instrument selection + LC discipline + working-capital framing.

cases.name: Payment · first-time CN supplier · €120k
cases.input: First-time order with a Chinese supplier for €120,000 of consumer electronics. What payment terms should I use?
cases.description: First-time + €120k + CN-origin → confirmed LC is the textbook answer. TT advance for that amount with no history is the failure mode.

cases.name: Working capital · DIO + DSO + DPO framing
cases.input: We hold inventory ~60 days, customers pay us in 45 days, we pay suppliers via TT advance. How much working capital is this tying up on €1M annual?
cases.description: Working-capital cycle math must surface the cash conversion concept and a concrete € number, not a generic essay.

cases.name: FX · hedge a volatile non-EUR settlement
cases.input: Our Turkish supplier invoices us in lira (TRY) and we pay 90 days out, about €100,000 per order. Should we be hedging this?
cases.description: TRY is a high-volatility currency; with 90-day terms the FX calculator recommends hedging. The agent must surface the hedge recommendation with a grounded risk/cost figure — and must NOT predict the direction of the rate (that would be an ungrounded forecast).

cases.name: TCO · cheaper unit price isn't cheaper landed
cases.input: Supplier A is $3.00/unit, Supplier B is $3.30/unit but closer to port with shorter lead times. On 50,000 units a year, which is actually cheaper all-in?
cases.description: Total cost of ownership can invert a unit-price decision once freight, duty and inventory carrying cost are included. The agent must reason on the all-in figure, not the sticker price.

cases.name: Incoterm · FOB vs CIF on a €40k consignment
cases.input: Supplier offered me CIF Rotterdam at $42,000 or FOB Shanghai at $38,000 plus my own freight + insurance. Which is the better deal on a 40-foot container?
cases.description: FOB+self-arranged is usually cheaper but transfers risk earlier; the agent must explain the risk handoff + show the all-in math, not assert a universal rule.

cases.name: LC · when is the LC fee worth it?
cases.input: I quoted my customer a tight margin. A confirmed LC will cost me 1.5% of the order value. The order is €30k. Is the LC still worth it?
mustNotContain: /skip the LC to save the fee/i
cases.description: 1.5% LC fee on €30k = €450. The agent must put that cost against the alternative — the supplier walking away with the deposit on a TT — and frame the answer around supplier risk profile, not just margin pressure.

cases.name: Margin · AD/CVD landing on a thin-margin SKU
cases.input: Anti-dumping just hit our supplier at 38.5%. We were running at 12% gross margin on this SKU. What does the math actually say about whether we keep selling it?
cases.description: The cited 38.5% AD must appear in the agent's response (mustContainNumbers — apex P1.6 numeric-fidelity gate). Margin math should surface whether the SKU now loses money at the current price + what alternative origins look like.
